Anyone working within the Northern City Line Tunnels should wear respiratory protection to a minimum of EN149 FF2. The correct use of a dust mask will meet these requirements.
Asbestos cement ceiling tiles to link corridors. Asbestos cement covered cables.
Walking route is off York Way then along route parallel to the box entrance.

All tracks have overhead 25,000-volt AC electric traction supply cables. All works undertaken on the station must be carried out in accordance with the Network Rail instructions concerning personal safety on electrified lines
There are no 3rd rail electric conductor rails at this station.
There are no 4th rail electric conductor rails at this station.

Requirement to disturb the surface within the station's controlled area, the Buried Services Procedure shall be followed at all times. Work will not be allowed to commence until such time that a Permit to Dig/Penetrate form has been duly signed by the Contract Administrator. Numerous low voltage cables are located underground throughout the station. Each contractor will be responsible for scanning of the work site in order to locate the exact location of the underground power cables.

In addition to the above cabling, London Kings Cross contains a large quantity of operational equipment including Signalling and Telecommunications <S&T> cables and train dispatch equipment. Any contractor proposing to undertake any demolition, excavation or works that could disturb any cables should refer to the National Hazard Directory. Additional information on S&T equipment can be obtained from the Route Signalling Engineer.

A Lightning Protection System is installed to all roof areas and extraneous metal surfaces. The system comprises flat aluminium continuity tapes linked to copper clad earth rods, located around the perimeter of all buildings. Any temporary or permanent works to the system must be agreed in advance with he Senior Asset Manager.
Records of the drainage layout is held by the Senior Asset Manager and the Facility Delivery Manager. Contractors should note that drainage exists beneath platforms and concourse areas.

Records of water supply pipelines are held by Senior Asset Manager and the Facility Delivery Manager, domestic supply and Fire Mains <now redundant> are known to run below Platforms 1 &11 with sub-mains area present in most areas.

Records of gas supply pipelines are held by the Senior Asset Manager and the Facility Delivery Manager. All mains gas supply pipes up to and including the meter are the responsibility of National Grid. In case of emergency contact 0800 111 999. Natural gas is non-toxic, but large concentrations could cause suffocation. It is highly flammable and leakage from a pipeline could result in an explosive mixture with air. Noise of escaping gas could be sufficient to cause damage to hearing and ear protection must be worn. All persons must be kept 100 metres from the damaged pipeline.
Asbestos hazards please see ARMS for specific details
There is a need to be aware of the potential for vagrants, prostitutes and needles etc in public areas.

No section of London Kings Cross Major Station is classed as a sub surface station. It should however be noted that the London Underground concourse and the platforms of the Metropolitan, Northern, Victoria and Piccadilly Lines and passages thereto are designated as sub-surface.

HV & LV rooms for Western Range are located in LB04 to LB10 <NR Plant Room>. Entry into the HV area must be authorised via station SSM and upon showing void Level C access card.2011. Free Text: Special sets of Insulated Rail Joints <IRJ> exist at each end of the incline single line. No train or vehicle should stand over these IRJ simultaneously; however, it is possible for legitimate non-conflicting routes to be established from Kings Cross <KX> and Camden Road Signalboxes which would enable this situation.~ To avoid this, the KX signaller must immediately be advised if a train is positioned, or to be moved, over the IRJ between Track Circuits <TC> T1232 & T1213 at Camden Road or if the IRJ between these TC fails. The KX signaller must not then allow a movement across the IRJ between TC XNB & T.007 on KX panel.~ Equally, if KX advises that a train is positioned or to be moved over the IRJ between <TC> XNB & T.007 or that the IRJ has failed, no movement is to be authorised across the IRJ between TC T1232 & T1213 at Camden Road~ Special arrangements will need to be considered by the Possession Planner/PICOP if one or more of these IRJ?s are included within a Possession.

Free Text: Maintenance access point for all types including Road Rail vehicles for the Canal Tunnels, the access route is off York Way through a wayleave that crosses Argent Land. There will always be 3.5m clearance for this access. To allow access with vehicles there needs to be an isolation of the new Canel Tunnels lines but also an isolation of the Up&Down slows on the ECML. The RRAP has a fairly tight curve and steep gradient <1:40> so extra care must be taken when accessing machines down there. Metal drainage cover also located on the slope is a potential hazard due to lack of surface traction especially in wet weather conditions. Within the Argent Land a parking provision for 1 no. van is available but no machines must be left within the wayleave access across Argent land. Machines must access on the night and not be stored there.

Gas main running from the junction of Hampden Rd to the depot train shed. It runs underground up Hampden Rd to a meter house near the depot entrance. It is generally buried at 600mm depth, but rises to 200mm within a steel duct where it crosses Hampden Rd Bridge ECM1/24A. The section of gas main along Hampden Rd up to the meter house belongs to Transco. From the meter house at the depot entrance the gas main runs above ground attached to a building and underground in ducting up the main train shed heating system and admin block boiler room. Reference drawing C816/400/001R4, C816/400/007R1 & C816/400/008R1 in the Network Rail LNET Records Centre.

It is illegal to intentionally capture, injure or kill badgers in their sett, damage, destroy or obstruct access to a badger sett. The guidelines on the permitted working distance from badger setts should be followed: Using very heavy machinery within 30m of any entrance to an active sett. Or lighter machinery. i.e. for any digging operation. within 20m. Or light work, such as digging with hand tools or scrub clearance within 10m. ALL ACTIVITIES REQUIRE A LICENCE. All staff to be made aware via tool box talks. Further information can be found using the BAP. Biodiversity Action Plan.
